title: Dubliners
author: James Joyce
excerpt: Fifteen stories that capture the lives of ordinary Dubliners at the turn of the century—each a portrait of paralysis, yearning, and the sudden flash of insight Joyce called 'epiphany.'
keyIdeas: Paralysis is the central condition of modern urban life—the inability to act, to change, to escape
Epiphany—a sudden moment of spiritual revelation—is the true subject of fiction
The details of everyday life, rendered with precision, carry the deepest meaning
Leaving home is the central drama of growing up, and it almost never happens
keyTakeaways: Small moments can reveal entire lives—pay attention to the flash of insight
The hardest cage to escape is the one we carry inside us
Joyce showed that the local is universal: Dublin is everywhere
Growing up means recognizing that the world will not accommodate our dreams
whoShouldRead: Readers who love the short story form and want to see it transformed
Anyone who has ever felt trapped by their circumstances and longed to escape
Writers interested in how precise language can create profound emotional effects
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking conventional plots with dramatic action and resolution
Those who find Joyce's later work (Ulysses) impenetrable but hope this is easier
Readers who dislike stories about disappointment and failed dreams
